ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Murgh Lababdar. FOODON_03302757 (chicken curry) is the closest parent class covering spiced chicken dishes in sauce.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Murgh Lababdar or Mughlai chicken curry code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Murgh Lababdar or a sufficiently specific Indian chicken curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Murgh Lababdar is approximately 300 g, comprising roasted chicken pieces in a rich almond-tomato cream sauce, usually served with naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken curry, restaurant-style (North Indian) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for chicken, cream, almond, and tomato-based curry sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Murgh Lababdar is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for North Indian chicken curry (roasted chicken in spiced tomato-cream gravy) and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy values for key ingredients (chicken breast/thigh, heavy cream, ground almonds, tomato puree, butter, onion, spices). The dish is a Mughlai-style preparation with moderate fat from cream and almonds. Values represent a weighted composite estimate per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat-based curry dishes; analogous estimate based on chicken curry (GI ~35) from the University of Sydney GI Database and published literature on low-carbohydrate Indian meat curries
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Murgh Lababdar exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with chicken curry entries (GI range 30–40) in the Sydney GI Database, reflecting the low net carbohydrate content (~4.8 g/100 g) from tomato, onion, and spices with negligible starch. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 14.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 5.0. This is consistent with published data on low-GI meat-based Indian curries.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg/100 g; ~960 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Murgh Lababdar provides approximately 960 mg sodium (about 42% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), so individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-salt preparation, and balance the meal with low-sodium accompaniments such as plain rice rather than salted naan.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low net carbohydrate content (~4.8 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Lababdar has a low glycemic index (~35) and low carbohydrate density, resulting in a glycemic load of approximately 5 per serving, making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled and served with a low-GI staple such as basmati rice or whole-wheat roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cream and butter (~3.8 g/100 g; ~11.4 g per serving) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The cream and butter in Murgh Lababdar contribute approximately 11.4 g of saturated fat per 300 g serving, which represents over half the recommended daily limit of 20 g for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should request lighter cream preparation, limit portion size, and pair the dish with fibre-rich vegetables to mitigate lipid impact.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Lababdar is a classic North Indian Mughlai chicken dish in which roasted or tandoor-cooked chicken pieces are simmered in a rich, velvety sauce made from tomatoes, onions, cream, butter, and ground almonds, seasoned with aromatic spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, ginger, and garlic. The name 'Lababdar' derives from the Urdu word conveying richness and indulgence. The sauce is characteristically smooth, mildly spiced, and slightly sweet from the almonds and cream, with a deep orange-red colour from the tomato base. The texture is thick and coating, and the chicken is tender from the roasting and slow simmering process. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 148 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 9.2 g fat (of which 3.8 g is saturated), and 4.8 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-calorie, protein-rich dish with relatively low carbohydrate content. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management when portion-controlled. The primary clinical considerations are the moderate sodium content (~320 mg/100 g) relevant to hypertension management, and the saturated fat from cream and butter relevant to cardiovascular health. The dish is naturally gluten-free and is typically halal. It is commonly served in Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent, often accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is popular in Mughlai, North Indian, and Pakistani restaurant menus globally.

meal_context: paired with naan or basmati rice

ingredient_synonyms.lababdar: rich, indulgent (Urdu — referring to the sauce style)
ingredient_synonyms.ghee: clarified butter (sometimes substituted for butter in this dish)

confidence_reason: Murgh Lababdar is not listed as a discrete entry in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN North Indian chicken curry data and USDA SR Legacy ingredient-level data. GI is estimated by analogy with chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the dish composition is well-documented in culinary literature and the ingredient-level nutrient estimation is grounded in approved sources.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from ICMR-NIN (North Indian chicken curry) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy ingredient data; no single approved-source entry exists for Murgh Lababdar as a discrete dish. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN or USDA SR Legacy sources used for this estimation. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary sources used for this composite estimate. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on chicken curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Murgh Lababdar exists. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (35 × 14.4 g available carbohydrate per 300 g serving) / 100 ≈ 5.0. 6. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry (Boat Quay, Singapore) per field survey data. 7. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context and typical preparation; kosher status is not confirmed. 8. Almond and dairy allergens are present; individuals with tree nut or dairy allergies should avoid this dish.
